title: Are Naoko and Ritsuko antagonists
wikitext:
[]: Are Naoko and Ritsuko antagonists?             Quote Post by Stryker » Sun Oct 23, 2011 1:26 pm I have been recently watching in the "Least favorite character" thread hear the Akagi family be disliked significantly. Now, of course, that doesn't really mean anything in this topic, but this is still worth putting up. Are the Akagi family antagonists?

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Sun Oct 23, 2011 1:39 pm I think they are both antagonists and victims. They both get used by Gendo quite a bit.

[]:             Quote Post by bobbyfischer's ghost » Sun Oct 23, 2011 1:47 pm ^ their victims in the sense that gendo used them and then threw them away. not that he doesn't do that with everything. To Shinji they are antagonist. two word's fish tank

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Sun Oct 23, 2011 1:53 pm Ritsuko certainly qualifies. Naoko, since she never even meets Shinji, might be a bit more of a stretch. The problem is that Shinji, being Shinji, is kind of his own primary antagonist, and if that doesn't work, everyone else in the world is kind of also his primary antagonist. Ancient Greek drama conventions only sort of apply to Japanese cartoons.

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Sun Oct 23, 2011 2:38 pm So, would that make them both tragic antagonists? (as apposed to a tragic hero)

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Sun Oct 23, 2011 3:11 pm Had we not already come to the conclusion that nearly all the characters fit in neither the protagonist nor antagonist group and that they are all simply people, characters trapped and tragically victimized by themselves and the world around them? Obviously this doesn't fit for all the characters, such as Kiel and Kensuke (damn normies), but for the most part it's prevalent in each character.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sun Oct 23, 2011 3:22 pm Sachi wrote: Had we not already come to the conclusion that nearly all the characters fit in neither the protagonist nor antagonist group and that they are all simply people, characters trapped and tragically victimized by themselves and the world around them? Obviously this doesn't fit for all the characters, such as Kiel and Kensuke (damn normies), but for the most part it's prevalent in each character.

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Sun Oct 23, 2011 3:50 pm Everyday people can be antagonists, as well as protagonists. Just in their own way, and the way they're shown. For example, Darth Vader started out as slave Anakin, who was the protagonist, who was trapped and victimized by the idea that he wanted to become a Jedi, and that he could never become one (until Qui-Gon showed up). That doesn't change the fact that he becomes the antagonist. Now, lets take it into Evangelion. Naoko was victimized by the fact that she was useless, and would be replaced by Rei (or something like that, it doesn't matter). That doesn't change the fact that she killed Rei, which was a bad thing to do. Remember that right and wrong are exactly the same as good and bad. Naoko did a bad thing, and antagonized. Therefore, she was an antagonist... right? I mean, it is all your perspective. Same thing for Ritsuko, for the same reason, and doing the same thing (except instead of truly killing Rei, she destroyed a bunch of bodies and destroyed the chance of Rei being reincarnated, should she be killed). Right thing to do? Your choice. Oh, and that whole "missing the point of the exercise" crap: Yeah, we all know it is, but it kills discussion (which is never that bad). So, lets put it aside and talk about it. If it helps, let me rephrase this: Does the Akagi's positive outlay outweigh their negative outlay, or vice versa?

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Sun Oct 23, 2011 4:00 pm That's not even what antagonist/protagonist distinction is about, and the answer to such a question relies hugely on the morality opinions of whoever's answering. Right thing to do? Your choice.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sun Oct 23, 2011 4:16 pm Stryker wrote: Oh, and that whole "missing the point of the exercise" crap: Yeah, we all know it is, but it kills discussion (which is never that bad). So, lets put it aside and talk about it.

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Sun Oct 23, 2011 8:14 pm @Azathoth: Well, since my view of antagonist/protagonist distinction is so off, could you tell me what it is? Bagheera wrote: The problem is that there's nothing to talk about. I'm not being dismissive here: the roles of protagonist and antagonist are matters of convention, storytelling shortcuts meant to tell the audience who they should root for and find sympathetic in a very brief span of time. The problem is that they're extremely limited; real people aren't like that. Your better shows work to add layers to their characters, making the antagonists more sympathetic and the protagonists more flawed, but even that is making the best of a limited set of tools.

[]:             Quote Post by Trajan » Mon Oct 24, 2011 1:15 pm Protagonist and antagonist are overly simplistic terms for a series as morally ambiguous as Eva. On a personal note: do I like them? No.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Mon Oct 24, 2011 1:33 pm Stryker wrote: @Azathoth: Well, since my view of antagonist/protagonist distinction is so off, could you tell me what it is?

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon Oct 24, 2011 3:23 pm The few times Ritsuko is against Shinji and Misato (the overall best candidates for protagonist) it's because they're the ones in the wrong at the time, usually because they don't have all the facts or their judgement is clouded by strong emotion. If anything she's actually a better friend to them then they deserve.

[]:             Quote Post by ReiAyanami25 » Mon Oct 24, 2011 6:53 pm I view both members of the Akagi family to be antagonists. Dispite being used by Gendo, they both are perfectly harmless until they make bad choices, such as strangling toddlers or destroying clones in tanks. Plus, Ritsuko grows more and more selfish and whiney towards the end of the series, and attemping to kill her superiors and blowing up HQ for her own selfish jealousies and hurt feelings cannot be justified. But it is mostly Naoko that annoys me, namely for killing Rei one and making moves on Gendo after Yui had died. She was very selfish, so I believe she did not deserve to live, and neither did Ritsuko. She stirred up too much trouble.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon Oct 24, 2011 7:08 pm Naoko was a lonely woman who wanted love. Is it so unreasonable she'd snap when told she was just a used up old piece of garbage now that he had what he wanted? She clearly didn't intend to kill Rei, and was horrified at what she'd done as soon as she came to her senses. Ritsuko was pretty much the same only with an even better reason to go a bit haywire. The clones she destroys aren't actually people, just human-shaped objects. By the time she puts her 'blow everything up' plan into place it was a rather certain thing that the situation was hopeless anyway, so why should she let either Gendo or the committee get what they want? They've got much stronger claims on the role of antagonist then she does and are the direct causes of her breakdown.

[]:             Quote Post by ReiAyanami25 » Mon Oct 24, 2011 7:35 pm NemZ wrote: Is it so unreasonable she'd snap when told she was just a used up old piece of garbage now that he had what he wanted? She clearly didn't intend to kill Rei, and was horrified at what she'd done as soon as she came to her senses.

[]:             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Mon Oct 24, 2011 7:43 pm ReiAyanami25 wrote: It is still a extremely disgusting way to let out your anger, and it is one of those rare, extreme examples of poor anger management and impulsiveness. As for Naoko and Ritsuko being used, I agree that it was cruel of Gendo to treat them like that, and I do pity them. So I suppose they are not as bad as they are made out to be.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Mon Oct 24, 2011 8:32 pm Well, if Ritsuko's the antagonist , who's the protagonist ? If you were to sum-up the entire show in one sentence it would sound a lot like this: "A timid 14-year-old boy named Shinji Ikari is faced with the fearful task of destroying the invading Angels by using the ultimate battle-weapon, Evangelion Unit 01." This would make Shinji to be the protagonist of the series. So in order for Ritsuko to be the antagonist, she would need to thwart Shinji's task of beating the Angels. But she doesn't do this at any one point in the show. Not even her destroying the Dummy Plug in Episode 23 was an attempt to keep Shinji from his main task. In fact, her destroying the Dummy Plug System had no effect on Shinji's ability to pilot the Evangelion. (I say "task" and not "goal" because Shinji made it his "goal" to never run away in the TV series, which helped him out greatly when achieving his "task".) But ... at the same time she showed Shinji horrible, horrible things in Episode 23. The show was never clear if she meant to make the poor boy run away again, therefore thwarting both his goal of never running away and his task of destroying the Angels all in one foul swoop. If she was attempting to scar Shinji into running away again, this would still make her an antagonist in the show, even though she kinda failed at it. (Fails just like her mother...)

[]:             Quote Post by ReiAyanami25 » Mon Oct 24, 2011 8:48 pm xanderkh wrote: They're all so psychologically broken by each other that it's hard to define who exactly is the antagonist or protagonist.

Now, of course, that doesn't really mean anything in this topic, but this is still worth putting up. Are the Akagi family antagonists?
